section.title: The Triumphal Entry and the Temple
section.sectionRationale: The long journey ends. Jesus rides into Jerusalem on a borrowed colt while the crowds spread their cloaks and hail Him as the King who comes in the name of the Lord, and when the Pharisees object, He says that if these were silent the very stones would cry out. Yet as He sees the city He weeps, for it did not recognize the time of its visitation and judgment is coming. Entering the temple, He drives out the sellers, declaring that God's house of prayer has been made a den of robbers, and He teaches there daily while the leaders plot His death.
section.thesis: Jesus comes to Jerusalem as her humble King, hailed with words He accepts as His due, yet He weeps over a city blind to her visitation and cleanses the temple as its Lord, so that the King's arrival brings both salvation offered and judgment warned.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the Triumphal Entry on the colt; Jesus weeping over Jerusalem; and the cleansing of the temple with the daily teaching that provokes the leaders.

units.label: The Triumphal Entry
units.note: Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord

verses.text: After Jesus had said this, He went on ahead, going up to Jerusalem.
As He approached Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, He sent out two of His disciples,
saying, “Go into the village ahead of you, and as you enter it, you will find a colt tied there, on which no one has ever sat. Untie it and bring it here.
If anyone asks, ‘Why are you untying it?’ tell him, ‘The Lord needs it.’”
So those who were sent went out and found it just as Jesus had told them.
As they were untying the colt, its owners asked, “Why are you untying the colt?”
“The Lord needs it,” they answered.
Then they led the colt to Jesus, threw their cloaks over it, and put Jesus on it.
As He rode along, the people spread their cloaks on the road.
And as He approached the descent from the Mount of Olives, the whole multitude of disciples began to praise God joyfully in a loud voice for all the miracles they had seen:
“Blessed is the {{basileus|King}} who comes in the name of the Lord!” “Peace in heaven and glory in the highest!”
But some of the Pharisees in the crowd said to Him, “Teacher, rebuke Your disciples!”
“I tell you,” He answered, “if they remain silent, the very stones will cry out.”

units.label: Jesus weeps over Jerusalem
units.note: you did not recognize the time of your visitation

verses.text: As Jesus approached Jerusalem and saw the city, He wept over it
and said, “If only you had known on this day what would bring you {{eirene|peace}}! But now it is hidden from your eyes.
For the days will come upon you when your enemies will barricade you and surround you and hem you in on every side.
They will level you to the ground, you and the children within your walls. They will not leave one stone on another, because you did not recognize the time of your {{episkope|visitation}} from God.”

units.label: The cleansing of the temple
units.note: My house will be a house of prayer

verses.text: Then Jesus entered the temple courts and began to drive out those who were selling there.
He declared to them, “It is written: ‘My house will be a house of {{proseuche|prayer}}.’ But you have made it ‘a den of robbers.’”
Jesus was teaching at the temple every day, but the chief priests, scribes, and leaders of the people were intent on killing Him.
Yet they could not find a way to do so, because all the people hung on His words.

basileus.gloss: King. Luke's crowd hails Jesus not merely as one who comes in the name of the Lord but as the King, drawing on Psalm 118 and Zechariah's promise of a king coming humble and riding on a colt. Jesus accepts the acclamation as His due, for He is entering His own royal city, though His throne will be a cross.

eirene.gloss: Peace, the wholeness of the Hebrew shalom. Jerusalem, whose very name echoes peace, did not know the things that make for peace on the day her King came to her. The peace He brings is not political freedom but reconciliation with God, offered and, tragically, refused.

episkope.gloss: Visitation, a coming to inspect, to help, or to judge. Jesus weeps because Jerusalem did not recognize the time of her visitation, the day God Himself drew near in His Son. To miss the visitation of grace is to be left to the visitation of judgment.

proseuche.gloss: Prayer. Quoting Isaiah, Jesus insists that God's house is to be a house of prayer, a place of meeting God for all peoples, but it had been turned into a den of robbers. As Lord of the temple He purges it, reclaiming it for its true purpose of communion with God.

basic.context: The long road to Jerusalem reaches its goal. Jesus sends two disciples to fetch a colt no one has ridden, and rides into the city as the crowds throw their cloaks on the road and praise God for all they have seen, crying, Blessed is the King who comes in the name of the Lord. When the Pharisees tell Him to silence the crowd, He says that if they were quiet the stones would cry out. But as the city comes into view, Jesus weeps, for Jerusalem did not know the things that make for peace and did not recognize the time of God's visitation; enemies will one day surround her and leave not one stone on another. Entering the temple, He drives out the sellers, declaring that God's house of prayer has been made a den of robbers, and He teaches there every day while the leaders look for a way to kill Him.
basic.hermeneutics: See the King arriving to claim His city, and hold together the joy and the grief. The entry is a deliberate, humble messianic claim that Jesus accepts. The tears and the temple cleansing reveal that His coming is a crisis: salvation for those who receive Him, judgment for a city and a worship that reject Him.
basic.originalLanguages: The crowd hails Jesus as basileus, King. He weeps because Jerusalem missed what makes for eirene, peace with God, failing to recognize her episkope, her visitation. And He cleanses the temple to restore it as a house of proseuche, prayer, rather than a den of robbers.
basic.typology: The King riding a colt fulfills Zechariah's prophecy of the humble king coming to Zion. The praise of Psalm 118, Blessed is He who comes, is claimed by Jesus. The cleansing of the temple enacts Malachi's promise that the Lord will suddenly come to His temple to purify worship, and points to Jesus as the true temple.
basic.christ: Jesus is the humble King who comes to His city on a borrowed colt, the Lord who needs it and receives it, and the One whose praise even stones would sing. He is God visiting His people in person, weeping over them like the Lord over Israel of old, and the Lord of the temple who purges His Father's house.
basic.god: God comes near in His Son to save, and His visitation demands recognition and welcome. He grieves over those who will not know their day of peace, yet His justice is real, and He is jealous for the purity of His house, which is meant for prayer and communion with all peoples.
basic.crossReferences: Zechariah 9 foretells the king coming humble on a colt. Psalm 118 supplies Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord. Isaiah 56 calls the temple a house of prayer for all nations. Malachi 3 promises the Lord suddenly coming to His temple.
basic.oneStory: The journey ends with the King entering His city, welcomed and yet rejected, hailed by disciples and wept over by their Lord. The story turns here toward the cross: the visitation of grace is offered, the temple is claimed, and a people who will not know their peace set themselves to kill the King who came to bring it.

context.literary: This is the climax of Luke's long travel narrative, the arrival at Jerusalem that has been anticipated since chapter 9. The entry, the lament, and the temple cleansing form a threefold coming of the King to His city and His house, and they open the final week that will lead to the passion. Luke uniquely records the weeping over the city and the retort about the stones.
context.historical: Riding a colt into Jerusalem at Passover, amid crowds swollen for the feast, was a charged messianic act echoing Zechariah. Spreading cloaks was a gesture of royal welcome. The temple's outer courts had become a market for sacrificial animals and currency exchange, and the prophecy of encircling enemies and leveled walls was fulfilled in the Roman siege of AD 70.
context.canonical: The humble king of Zechariah, the pilgrim praise of Psalm 118, the house of prayer for all nations in Isaiah, and the purifying Lord of Malachi all converge on this entry. The temple Jesus cleanses points forward to His own body as the true temple, and to the church and the new creation as the dwelling of God with His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present Jesus' deliberate arrival as the humble messianic King who accepts the worship due Him, to reveal His grief and warning over a city that will not recognize its visitation, and to show Him as Lord of the temple purging His Father's house for its true purpose of prayer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The entry, the lament, and the cleansing are narrated events at the turning point of the Gospel. They are not commands, but they carry weighty implications: recognize and welcome the King, know the things that make for peace, and honor God's house and its worship rather than corrupting it.

debates.question: What kind of king is Jesus claiming to be by this entry?

views.label: The humble, peaceable King of Zechariah's prophecy
views.case: By choosing a colt rather than a warhorse, Jesus enacts Zechariah 9:9, the king coming gentle and bringing peace, not a military deliverer. He accepts royal acclamation but redefines kingship around humility and a peace with God that the crowd, longing for political rescue, largely misunderstood.

views.label: A provocative public claim to be Messiah and Lord
views.case: The staged fulfillment of prophecy, the acceptance of Psalm 118's praise, and the refusal to silence the crowd amount to an open messianic declaration in the capital at Passover, a claim the authorities rightly perceived as a direct challenge, hastening their plot to kill Him.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: Jesus openly claims to be the promised King and Lord, yet a humble one whose peace is reconciliation with God and whose throne will be a cross.

debates.question: Why does Jesus cleanse the temple, and what does it signify?

views.label: A reclaiming of true worship against corruption
views.case: Quoting Isaiah and Jeremiah, Jesus condemns the turning of a house of prayer for all nations into a den of robbers, an exploitative market. His action reforms worship, insisting the temple exists for communion with God, not commerce and profit.

views.label: An enacted sign of judgment on the temple system
views.case: Coming right after His prophecy of the city's destruction, the cleansing functions as a prophetic sign that the temple's days are numbered and that Jesus Himself is now the true meeting place with God. It is less a reform than a verdict on a worship that will be replaced.

debates.bearing: Whether reform or verdict, the point stands: Jesus is Lord of the temple, jealous for real worship, and in Him God's true house of prayer is being established.

study.originalLanguages: The disciples hail Jesus as basileus, King, borrowing Psalm 118 and echoing Zechariah's promise of a king who comes humble, riding on a colt, and Jesus receives the acclamation rather than deflecting it, for He is entering His own royal city. Yet at the sight of Jerusalem He weeps, because she did not know the things that make for eirene, the shalom of reconciliation with God, mistaking her hope for political rescue. The reason for her ruin, He says, is that she did not recognize the time of her episkope, her visitation, the day God Himself drew near in His Son to help and to save; to miss the visitation of grace is to be handed over to the visitation of judgment. And entering the temple He acts as its Lord, purging it so that it may again be a house of proseuche, prayer, the place of meeting God for all peoples, rather than the den of robbers it had become.

typology.type: The humble king coming to Zion on a colt, Zechariah 9:9
typology.fulfillment: Jesus riding into Jerusalem on a colt never before ridden, Luke 19:35-38.

typology.type: The Lord suddenly coming to His temple to purify, Malachi 3:1-3
typology.fulfillment: Jesus entering the temple and driving out the sellers, Luke 19:45-46.

typology.type: The temple as a house of prayer for all nations, Isaiah 56:7
typology.fulfillment: Jesus reclaiming the temple's purpose and pointing to Himself as the true meeting place with God, Luke 19:46.

study.christ: Jesus is the King who comes at last to His capital, not on a warhorse but on a borrowed colt, humble and bringing peace, exactly as Zechariah foretold. He is the Lord who needs the colt and receives it, and whose praise is so fitting that were the crowd silenced the very stones would cry out. He is God visiting His people, weeping over the city as the LORD wept over faithless Israel, longing to give a peace they would not receive. And He is the Lord of the temple, entering His Father's house to purge it and to reclaim it for prayer, even as the guardians of that house set themselves to kill Him. In every scene He is the sovereign, sorrowing, cleansing King.
study.god: God draws near in the entry of His Son, a visitation of grace that calls for recognition and welcome. He is a God who weeps over the lost, whose heart breaks for a city that will not know its day of peace, and whose warnings of judgment are not cold threats but the grief of spurned love. He is jealous for the honor of His house and the purity of worship, insisting that His dwelling be a place of prayer and communion for all peoples rather than a market for profit. This is a God who comes to save and who must be received, for to refuse His visitation is to invite His judgment.

crossReferences.note: Behold, your king comes to you, humble and riding on a colt, fulfilled in the Triumphal Entry.
Blessed is he who comes in the name of the LORD, the cry the crowds raise to Jesus the King.
My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations, the word Jesus invokes in cleansing the temple.
The Lord will suddenly come to His temple to purify, enacted as Jesus drives out the sellers.

study.oneStory: The long journey ends as the humble King rides into His city, welcomed by disciples and wept over by their Lord, who sees that Jerusalem will not know the peace He brings. He enters the temple as its rightful Lord, purging it for prayer, and teaches there daily while the leaders plot His death. Here the whole Gospel pivots toward the cross: the visitation of grace is offered and the King's own house is claimed, and a people blind to their day of peace move to kill the very One who came to give it.

quiz.q: How does Jesus enter Jerusalem, and what does the crowd cry?

options.text: On a colt, while the crowds spread their cloaks and hail Him as the King who comes in the name of the Lord.
options.note: Luke 19:35-38 shows the humble royal entry and the messianic acclamation.

options.text: Riding into the city as the people praise God for His miracles.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific hail of Him as King and the royal cloak-spreading.

options.text: On foot, quietly slipping into the city unnoticed.
options.note: The entry is public and royal, on a colt with loud praise, not a quiet arrival.

options.text: On a warhorse, leading an armed procession.
options.note: He comes on a colt, the humble king of Zechariah, not a military conqueror.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say when the Pharisees tell Him to silence the crowd?

options.text: That if the disciples were silent, the very stones would cry out.
options.note: Luke 19:40 affirms that His praise is so fitting creation itself would voice it.

options.text: That the praise of His disciples is entirely appropriate.
options.note: True, but it misses the vivid claim that even the stones would cry out.

options.text: That He would ask the crowd to quiet down a little.
options.note: He refuses to silence them, insisting the praise cannot be stopped.

options.text: That the Pharisees were right to object.
options.note: He rejects their objection outright.

quiz.q: Why does Jesus weep over Jerusalem?

options.text: Because the city did not know the things that make for peace or recognize the time of its visitation from God.
options.note: Luke 19:42-44 gives both the missed peace and the unrecognized visitation.

options.text: Because judgment and destruction were coming upon the city.
options.note: True, but the root cause He names is that they missed their day of peace and God's visitation.

options.text: Because the people did not welcome Him with enough enthusiasm.
options.note: The crowds did welcome Him; His grief is over the city's deeper blindness to its visitation.

options.text: Because He feared what the leaders would do to Him.
options.note: His tears are for the city's blindness and coming ruin, not for Himself.

quiz.q: What does Jesus do in the temple, and how does He explain it?

options.text: He drives out the sellers, saying God's house of prayer has been made a den of robbers.
options.note: Luke 19:45-46 records the cleansing and the quotation of Isaiah and Jeremiah.

options.text: He clears out those who were selling in the temple courts.
options.note: True, but it omits His words explaining that the house of prayer had become a den of robbers.

options.text: He rebukes the priests for their long prayers.
options.note: The issue is the corruption of the temple market, not the length of prayers.

options.text: He praises the temple commerce as good service to pilgrims.
options.note: The opposite: He condemns it and drives the sellers out.

quiz.q: What is the response of the leaders and the people to Jesus' daily teaching in the temple?

options.text: The leaders sought to kill Him, but could not, because all the people hung on His words.
options.note: Luke 19:47-48 sets the leaders' murderous intent against the people's rapt attention.

options.text: The leaders wanted Him dead but were held back for now.
options.note: True, but it omits why they were held back: the people were captivated by His teaching.

options.text: The people were indifferent to His teaching.
options.note: The opposite: they hung on His every word.

options.text: The leaders welcomed His teaching in the temple.
options.note: They were intent on killing Him, not welcoming Him.

questions: Jesus accepted worship as the King who comes in the name of the Lord. Do you truly treat Him as King, or only as a helpful teacher, and where does His reign meet resistance in you?
The King wept over a city that did not know its day of peace. Are there people or places you have given up on that still move the heart of Christ to tears?
Jerusalem missed the time of its visitation. How might God be drawing near to you right now in ways you could fail to recognize?
Jesus cleansed His Father's house so it would be a place of prayer. What has crowded out prayer and true worship in your own life, and what needs to be driven out?
The people hung on His words while the leaders plotted against Him. When His teaching cuts across your plans, do you lean in to listen or look for a way around Him?

translationNotes.note: The acclamation quotes Psalm 118:26 and adds 'Peace in heaven and glory in the highest', echoing the angels' song at Jesus' birth. Luke alone calls the coming one 'the King', making the messianic claim explicit.

'They will level you to the ground, you and the children within your walls' translates a phrase set off in the printed text with an em dash, rendered here as a comma. The prophecy was fulfilled in the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem in AD 70.
Jesus combines Isaiah 56:7, 'My house will be a house of prayer', with Jeremiah 7:11, 'a den of robbers', condemning the corruption of temple worship and reclaiming the house for its true purpose.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 19:28-48. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, poem markers, and speaker labels were skipped when assembling verse text. The em dash in verse 44 was converted to a comma. Sermon status set to none; no sermon from the trusted cluster was verified this session.
